Q1: I usually organized my study routine around blocks of time when I could really get hands-on with the material. Being 27 and having been through various life experiences, I've learned that I need to physically engage with content—writing things out, practicing problems, even pacing while I review notes. I'd dedicate about four hours a day across the week, often in the evenings after other responsibilities. I found that consistency helped me stay grounded, even when the workload felt heavy.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shift things around. I tried to keep a steady rhythm—maybe studying Monday through Friday evenings—but when assignments piled up, I'd extend into weekends or wake up earlier. The core structure stayed the same though; I just added extra sessions when needed rather than abandoning my routine entirely.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS to check for new announcements or to access lecture videos and assignment details. Sometimes it was just habit—I'd wake up, grab my coffee, and check what was new. Other times, I'd log in specifically because I knew a deadline was approaching and I needed to submit something or review the rubric one more time.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging in became automatic for me. I think it happened because I made it part of my morning routine, right alongside checking messages and having breakfast. Once I associated it with that daily rhythm, it stopped feeling like a chore and just became something I did naturally. The consistency helped me stay on top of things without feeling overwhelmed.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I'd often watch a video while taking handwritten notes or working through examples alongside it. The discussion forums were less useful to me personally, but the assignment rubrics and feedback sections helped me understand exactly what was expected.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't engage much with the discussion posts—I only posted once the whole course. It's not that I didn't value peer perspectives, but I found more benefit in the peer interactions that happened naturally through study groups outside the LMS. Some of the supplementary readings I also skimmed rather than deeply engaged with, especially when time was tight.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to either understanding the material better or improving my grade. If I could apply what I learned immediately—like practicing a concept or completing a related assignment—it felt valuable. Activities that seemed disconnected from the core learning objectives or assessments felt less urgent to me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. With required work, there was a clear purpose and consequence, so I prioritized those. Optional materials I'd look at if I had extra time or if I was genuinely curious, but they often fell to the bottom of my list. It's not that they weren't valuable; it's just that with limited hours, I had to make choices.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and PDFs early in the course so I could study them offline. I'd print out key materials and annotate them by hand—that tactile engagement really helps me retain information. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks from the library to supplement what was provided in the course.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged in learning but the LMS wouldn't reflect it at all. For instance, I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper or reviewing printed notes while commuting. I also had informal study sessions with peers where we'd discuss concepts and quiz each other—none of that showed up in any log.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a video lecture once, download the accompanying slides, and then review those slides multiple times offline over the following days. The same with readings—I'd download them, print them out, and mark them up with highlighters and notes. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the hours of offline study that followed.
Q12: Around midterms, things got harder. I had some personal responsibilities that demanded attention, and the workload across all eight courses I was taking felt intense. There were a couple of weeks where I felt stretched thin, but I reminded myself why I was doing this and pushed through. My resilience kicked in—I've faced harder situations before.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities, I'd sometimes log into the LMS but not engage as deeply. I might skim through materials rather than taking detailed notes. Confusion with certain topics also slowed me down—I'd spend more time on those areas but sometimes had to move on before fully mastering them just to keep up.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open a video or reading but my mind was elsewhere. Maybe I was tired or preoccupied with something outside of school. I'd technically be 'watching' the video, but I wasn't absorbing much. Those sessions felt unproductive, and I'd often have to revisit the material later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed some assignments, honestly. My assignment completion rate wasn't where I wanted it to be. Sometimes I'd avoid starting because the task felt overwhelming, or I wasn't confident I understood the material well enough. Other times, I prioritized assignments from other courses that had earlier deadlines. I knew it was important, but I had to triage.
Q16: Deadlines were powerful motivators for me. I'd structure my LMS usage around them—checking due dates, planning my week accordingly. As deadlines approached, my activity would spike. I'd review materials more intensely, finalize assignments, and submit work. Without deadlines, I might have spread my effort too thin across everything.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me. When I received constructive comments on an assignment, I'd revisit those areas and try to improve for the next one. Positive feedback motivated me to keep pushing, while critical feedback helped me identify gaps. I appreciated instructors who gave timely, specific feedback—it made me feel like my effort mattered.
Q18: The course structure gave me a roadmap, which I appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my study time effectively. However, when multiple deadlines clustered together, it created pressure. Overall, the structure was supportive—I knew what was expected and when, which reduced uncertainty and helped me stay organized.
Q19: The video lectures being available on-demand made engagement easier because I could fit them into my schedule. However, some aspects were harder—like when discussion participation was expected but the forums felt inactive or disconnected from my learning style. More interactive, hands-on activities would have suited me better than text-based discussions.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to understand material but struggling to produce completed work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading resources, trying to grasp concepts—but when it comes to assignments, they hit a wall. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or time running out before they can finish.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was moderate, but I passed the course despite not completing all assignments. A lot of my learning happened offline—through printed materials, external resources, and study groups. The LMS activity doesn't capture that depth of engagement, so someone might look less active but still be learning effectively.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when students log in, what they click on, how long videos play. But they miss the quality of engagement. They can't tell if I was deeply focused or just had a tab open while doing something else. They also miss all offline learning, peer discussions, and the mental work of processing and connecting ideas.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively working to understand and apply the material. It's not just about logging in or watching videos—it's about wrestling with concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas to real-world applications. For me, engagement is hands-on, effortful, and purposeful. It's when learning feels alive, not passive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my mindfulness practice, which was part of my intervention and helped me manage stress and stay focused. It wouldn't capture the emotional resilience I brought to difficult weeks, or the satisfaction I felt when concepts finally clicked. It also wouldn't show the peer support I received and gave outside the platform—those human connections that kept me motivated.
